content:
# Leviticus Chapter 20 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 70r–70v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_020_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_020_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 106–107 | `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_020_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_020_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

*[Note on structure: Leviticus 20 functions as the penal companion to the prohibitions catalogued in Leviticus 18. Where chapter 18 stated what is forbidden — illicit sexual unions, child sacrifice to Molech, divination — chapter 20 assigns penalties. The chapter falls into five clear sections: (1) the capital crime of Molech-worship and the judicial response required of the community (vv.1–5); (2) the fate of those who turn to mediums and spirits of the dead (v.6); (3) a holiness exhortation framed as a covenantal summons (vv.7–8); (4) the death penalty for cursing parents (v.9); and (5) the extended penal code for sexual violations (vv.10–21), followed by a closing holiness exhortation grounding the law in the gift of the land and the call to separation from the nations (vv.22–26), and a final provision for the execution of mediums and necromancers (v.27). The chapter's rhetoric moves between divine first-person declarations ("I will set my face against," "I will cut off") and communal imperatives ("they shall stone him"), embedding individual divine response within a community's obligation to enforce holiness. The formulaic mot yumat ("he shall surely die") and ragom yirgemu ("they shall stone him") appear with relentless force through the central penal section. The chapter's closing vision — of a land "flowing with milk and honey" (eretz zavat chalav u-devash), whose enjoyment depends on covenant fidelity and separation from defiling practice — draws together the entire Holiness Code's logic: land-gift and land-loss are functions of the people's holiness or defilement. The chapter's final verse (v.27) returns to the topic of v.6, executing the practitioner of necromancy; the repetition forms a literary bracket around the entire chapter's concern with illegitimate access to the divine.]*

---

## Leviticus 20

**20:1** And the LORD spoke to Moshe, saying:

**20:2** "And to the sons of Yisra'el you shall say: Any man from the sons of Yisra'el and from the sojourner [ha-ger — הַגֵּר] who sojourns in Yisra'el who gives from his seed to the Molech [la-Molekh — לַמֹּלֶךְ] — he shall surely be put to death [mot yumat — מוֹת יוּמָת]; the people of the land [am ha-aretz — עַם הָאָרֶץ] shall stone him with stones [ragom yirgemu oto ba-aven — רָגוֹם יִרְגְּמוּ אֹתוֹ בָּאֶבֶן].

**20:3** And I — I will set my face [ani eten et-panai — אֲנִי אֶתֵּן אֶת-פָּנַי] against that man, and I will cut him off [ve-hikhratiti oto — וְהִכְרַתִּי אֹתוֹ] from among his people; for from his seed he gave to the Molech, so as to defile my sanctuary [le-tame et-miqdashi — לְטַמֵּא אֶת-מִקְדָּשִׁי] and to profane my holy name [le-challel et-shem qodshi — לְחַלֵּל אֶת-שֵׁם קׇדְשִׁי].

**20:4** And if the people of the land do at all hide their eyes [ha'lem ya'limu — הַעְלֵם יַעְלִימוּ] from that man when he gives from his seed to the Molech — so as not to put him to death —

**20:5** then I will set my face against that man and against his family [mishpachto — מִשְׁפַּחְתּוֹ], and I will cut him off and all who go whoring after him [ha-zonim acharav — הַזֹּנִים אַחֲרָיו], whoring after the Molech, from among their people.

**20:6** And the person [ha-nefesh — הַנֶּפֶשׁ] who turns to the mediums [ha-ovot — הָאֹבוֹת] and to the spirits of the dead [ve-el ha-yid'onim — וְאֶל הַיִּדְּעֹנִים], to go whoring after them — I will set my face against that person and I will cut him off from among his people.

**20:7** And you shall sanctify yourselves [ve-hitqadishtem — וְהִתְקַדִּשְׁתֶּם] and you shall be holy [vi-heyitem qedoshim — וִהְיִיתֶם קְדֹשִׁים], for I am the LORD your God.

**20:8** And you shall keep my statutes [et-chuqqotai — אֶת-חֻקֹּתַי] and you shall do them. I am the LORD who sanctifies you [meqadishkhem — מְקַדִּשְׁכֶם].

**20:9** For any man who curses [yeqallel — יְקַלֵּל] his father and his mother shall surely be put to death [mot yumat — מוֹת יוּמָת]; his father and his mother he has cursed — his blood is upon himself [damav bo — דָּמָיו בּוֹ].

**20:10** And a man who commits adultery [yena'ef — יְנָאֵף] with the wife of another man — who commits adultery with the wife of his neighbor — the adulterer [ha-na'ef — הַנֹּאֵף] and the adulteress [ha-na'afet — הַנֹּאָפֶת] shall surely be put to death [mot yumatu — מוֹת יוּמָתוּ].

**20:11** And a man who lies with the wife of his father [eshet aviv — אֵשֶׁת אָבִיו] — the nakedness of his father he has uncovered — both of them shall surely be put to death [mot yumatu — מוֹת יוּמָתוּ]; their blood is upon them [demeihem bam — דְּמֵיהֶם בָּם].

**20:12** And a man who lies with his daughter-in-law [kallato — כַּלָּתוֹ] — both of them shall surely be put to death; a confusion [tevel — תֶּבֶל] they have done; their blood is upon them.

**20:13** And a man who lies with a male [zachar — זָכָר] as one lies with a woman [mishkevei ishah — מִשְׁכְּבֵי אִשָּׁה] — an abomination [to'evah — תּוֹעֵבָה] they have done, both of them; they shall surely be put to death; their blood is upon them.

**20:14** And a man who takes a woman and her mother [imah — אִמָּהּ] — it is depravity [zimmah — זִמָּה]; in fire they shall burn him and them, and there shall not be depravity [zimmah] in your midst.

**20:15** And a man who gives his lying [shekhovto — שְׁכׇבְתּוֹ] to an animal [behemah — בְּהֵמָה] — he shall surely be put to death, and the animal you shall kill [ve-et ha-behemah taharegu — וְאֶת-הַבְּהֵמָה תַּהֲרֹגוּ].

**20:16** And a woman who approaches [tiqrav — תִּקְרַב] any animal so that it lies [le-riv'ah — לְרִבְעָהּ] with her — you shall kill the woman and the animal; they shall surely be put to death; their blood is upon them.

**20:17** And a man who takes his sister [achoto — אֲחֹתוֹ] — his father's daughter or his mother's daughter — and sees her nakedness and she sees his nakedness — it is a disgrace [chesed hu — חֶסֶד הוּא]; and they shall be cut off [ve-nikhreitu — וְנִכְרְתוּ] in the eyes of the sons of their people; the nakedness of his sister he has uncovered — his iniquity he shall bear [avono yissa — עֲוֺנוֹ יִשָּׂא].

**20:18** And a man who lies with a woman in the sickness of her impurity [dovah — דָּוָה] and uncovers her nakedness — her flow he has laid bare, and she has uncovered the flow of her blood — both of them shall be cut off from among their people [ve-nikhreitu shenehem mi-qerev amam — וְנִכְרְתוּ שְׁנֵיהֶם מִקֶּרֶב עַמָּם].

**20:19** And the nakedness of your mother's sister and of your father's sister you shall not uncover; for his own flesh [she'ero — שְׁאֵרוֹ] he has laid bare — they shall bear their iniquity [avonm yis'u — עֲוֺנָם יִשָּׂאוּ].

**20:20** And a man who lies with his uncle's wife [dodat — דֹּדָתוֹ] — the nakedness of his uncle he has uncovered — they shall bear their sin [chet'am yis'u — חֶטְאָם יִשָּׂאוּ]; childless [aririn — עֲרִירִים] they shall die.

**20:21** And a man who takes the wife of his brother [eshet achiv — אֵשֶׁת אָחִיו] — it is impurity [niddah — נִדָּה]; the nakedness of his brother he has uncovered — childless they shall be.

**20:22** And you shall keep all my statutes and all my judgments [mishpatai — מִשְׁפָּטַי] and you shall do them, and the land [ha-aretz — הָאָרֶץ] to which I am bringing you to dwell there shall not vomit you out [lo taqia — לֹא תָקִיא].

**20:23** And you shall not walk in the statutes of the nation [ba-chuqqot ha-goy — בְּחֻקֹּת הַגּוֹי] that I am driving out before you, for all these things they did, and I was loathed by them [va-aquts bam — וָאָקֻץ בָּם].

**20:24** And I said to you: You — you shall possess their ground [tirshу et-admatam — תִּירְשׁוּ אֶת-אַדְמָתָם], and I — I will give it to you to possess it, a land flowing with milk and honey [eretz zavat chalav u-devash — אֶרֶץ זָבַת חָלָב וּדְבַשׁ]. I am the LORD your God who distinguished you [hivdalti etkhem — הִבְדַּלְתִּי אֶתְכֶם] from the peoples.

**20:25** And you shall distinguish [ve-hivdaltem — וְהִבְדַּלְתֶּם] between the clean animal and the unclean [ben ha-behemah ha-tehorah la-teme'ah — בֵּין הַבְּהֵמָה הַטְּהֹרָה לַטְּמֵאָה] and between the unclean bird and the clean [bein ha-'of ha-tame le-tahor — בֵּין הָעוֹף הַטָּמֵא לַטָּהוֹר]; and you shall not make your souls detestable [lo teshaqtsu et-nafshoteikhem — לֹא תְשַׁקְּצוּ אֶת-נַפְשֹׁתֵיכֶם] by animal or by bird or by anything that creeps on the ground [ha-romes al ha-adamah — הָרֹמֵשׂ עַל-הָאֲדָמָה], which I have set apart [hivdalti — הִבְדַּלְתִּי] for you as unclean.

**20:26** And you shall be holy to me [qedoshim tihyu li — קְדֹשִׁים תִּהְיוּ לִי], for I the LORD am holy [qadosh — קָדוֹשׁ], and I have distinguished you from the peoples to be mine [lihyot li — לִהְיוֹת לִי].

**20:27** And a man or a woman in whom there is a medium [ov — אוֹב] or a spirit of the dead [yid'oni — יִדְּעֹנִי] — they shall surely be put to death [mot yumatu — מוֹת יוּמָתוּ]; with stones they shall stone them [ba-aven yirgemu otam — בָּאֶבֶן יִרְגְּמוּ אֹתָם]; their blood is upon them."

---

## Translator's Notes

### Structural Overview

Leviticus 20 is the judicial enforcement code of the Holiness Law, completing the legal architecture begun in chapter 18. Where chapter 18 enumerated the forbidden sexual unions (gilui arayot — the "uncovering of nakedness") and child sacrifice to Molech as categories of defilement, chapter 20 assigns the penalties. The chapter is structured in a ring that begins and ends with the topic of necromancy (vv.6 and 27), frames the central penal code within holiness exhortations (vv.7–8, 22–26), and advances from the most public crime (Molech-worship, requiring communal stoning) through private sexual violations. The dominant formula is the doubled mot yumat — the Niphal of mût intensified by its own infinitive absolute: "dying he shall die," rendered here "he shall surely be put to death." This formula appears five times (vv.9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 15, 16, 27), with the variation "their blood is upon them" (demeihem bam) serving as the rhetorical seal on each clause. That phrase — damav bo / demeihem bam — is a legal idiom of bloodguilt responsibility: the offender's death is his own doing; those who execute him bear no guilt.

The chapter's geography of penalty is notable. The most severe violations (Molech-worship, adultery, incest with father's wife, male-male intercourse, bestiality, daughter of a mother) receive mot yumat — the death sentence. A secondary tier (intercourse during menstrual impurity, sexual relations with aunts and uncles, with a brother's wife) receives kareth — being "cut off" from the people, a divine penalty of extirpation without human execution. A third tier (intercourse with an aunt by marriage or with a sister-in-law) receives the penalty of childlessness (aririn yamut — "they shall die childless"). This graduated structure reflects the chapter's underlying logic: violations are ranked by the degree of their threat to household, clan, and covenant community.

### Verse-by-Verse Notes

**v.2 — ha-ger ha-gar be-Yisra'el:** The explicit inclusion of the sojourner under the Molech prohibition is significant. The law of chapter 18:26 already stated that the ger is bound by the same prohibitions as the native Israelite. Chapter 20 applies the same capital penalty to both. The am ha-aretz ("people of the land") as the executing body is notable: this is not the priests or elders alone but the entire community that bears the obligation to enforce holiness.

**v.2 — ragom yirgemu:** The infinitive absolute of ragam ("to stone") intensifies the verb: "stoning they shall stone him." The communal stoning — a form of execution in which the entire community participates and thus bears collective moral responsibility for maintaining covenant holiness — is the standard biblical mode of execution for capital offenses against the covenant community. By stoning, no single individual "kills" the offender; the community as a whole expels the defilement.

**v.3 — ani eten et-panai:** "I will set my face against" is a divine idiom of hostile personal attention. The face (panim) of God turned toward a person could be a sign of blessing and favor (the Aaronic benediction: "the LORD make his face shine upon you," Num 6:25); turned against a person it signifies divine wrath and pursuit. The phrase appears three times in the chapter (vv.3, 5, 6), each time accompanying a pronouncement of divine cutting-off (kareth). The divine penalty supplements — and, where human courts fail (v.4), replaces — human execution.

**v.3 — le-tame et-miqdashi:** The theological logic of the Molech prohibition is here made explicit: child sacrifice to Molech defiles the sanctuary even though the act is performed outside it. The holiness of the sanctuary is a function of the holiness of the community that worships there. Sin committed anywhere in the covenant community radiates outward to defile the central cult. This "moral contagion" model of defilement — in which acts that are not intrinsically cultic nonetheless pollute the sacred space — is one of the Holiness Code's most distinctive theological contributions. It grounds the entire legal programme of Lev 17–26: the land and sanctuary are defiled by moral violations, not only ritual ones.

**v.4 — ha'lem ya'limu:** Another infinitive absolute construction: "hiding they shall hide [their eyes]" — a deliberate willful blindness, looking away from the Molech-worshipper to avoid the obligation of execution. The community's passive complicity is here treated as a legal failure with its own divine consequences (v.5: God himself will act against the neglectful community and the worshipper's entire mishpacha — "family/clan"). The word hivdalti ("I have set apart / distinguished") in v.24 connects: the community that refuses to distinguish (le-hivdil) between clean and unclean, holy and defiled, forfeits its own status as the "distinguished" people.

**v.5 — ha-zonim acharav:** "Those who go whoring after him" — the verb zanah (to go whoring/prostitute oneself) is used throughout the prophets for idolatry (cf. Ezek 6:9; Hos 1:2; Jer 3:6). The language of prostitution for covenant unfaithfulness is the metaphor of the covenant as marriage: Israel is the wife of YHWH, and devotion to other gods or cultic practices is marital infidelity. The "family" (mishpacha) of the offender is included in divine judgment, suggesting that clan solidarity in tolerating the violation implicates the whole household.

**v.6 — ha-nefesh asher tifneh:** The subject is ha-nefesh — the soul/person — who "turns" (tifneh, from panah, the same root as panim/face in vv.3,5: note the irony — the person turns their face toward the mediums; God turns his face against them). The mediums (ovot) and spirits of the dead (yid'onim) are the same pair forbidden in 19:31. Here, however, the penalty is explicitly divine cutting-off (kareth), rather than a community execution — suggesting this was a more private and harder-to-prosecute offense (contrast v.27, where the practitioner is executed).

**v.7 — ve-hitqadishtem vi-heyitem qedoshim:** A reflexive Hitpa'el ("sanctify yourselves") followed by a resulting state ("and you shall be holy"). The pairing distinguishes between active moral effort (the sanctification of conduct and practice) and the resulting ontological status (holiness as a quality of the community). The same formula appears in 11:44; 19:2; and the verb meqadishkhem in v.8 — "I am the LORD who sanctifies you" — makes the divine agency primary: human effort and divine gift are not alternatives but cooperating realities. The people sanctify themselves; the LORD is the one who sanctifies them.

**v.9 — mot yumat / damav bo:** The cursing of parents (qillel — Pi'el of qalal, "to treat as light/contemptible, to curse") is addressed first among the individual penalties, placed before the sexual offenses. The position may reflect the Decalogue sequence (honor of parents, Exod 20:12) or a theological logic: if a man curses the persons who stood in the place of God's authority in his formation, he has corrupted the deepest human order. The phrase damav bo — "his blood is upon himself" — appears first here and recurs through the chapter; it is a formula of self-incurred bloodguilt, meaning: the executioners bear no guilt for his death; he has brought it upon himself by his own act.

**v.10 — ha-na'ef ve-ha-na'afet:** Both the adulterer and the adulteress are named and penalized equally. The double mot yumat applies to both parties. This is noteworthy in the ancient Near Eastern context, where adultery laws elsewhere typically penalized only the woman (or gave the aggrieved husband discretion over both parties). The Levitical code here demands symmetrical execution, removing the husband's unilateral power of disposition.

**v.12 — tevel:** The term tevel for incest between a man and his daughter-in-law appears only here and in 18:23 (where it denotes bestiality). The root is obscure; it may connote "confusion" or "mixing" — a violation of the proper categorical distinctions that order family relations. It is not the standard Hebrew word for incest or abomination; its use here and in the bestiality law may group these as violations of natural categorical order.

**v.13 — mishkevei ishah:** The phrase is literally "the lyings of a woman" — meaning the sexual position or act as associated with a woman. The syntax is unusual and has been analyzed as an accusative of manner. The law prohibits a man from lying with a male in the way one lies with a female. Both partners receive the death penalty (both of them — sheneihem — they shall be put to death). The word to'evah ("abomination") appears here as in 18:22; it is a term of strong cultic-moral disqualification, used also for prohibited foods (Deut 14:3), dishonest weights (Deut 25:16), and pagan practices generally.

**v.14 — ishah ve-et imah:** Taking a woman and her mother is here called zimmah — the same word used in 19:29 for the depravity that results from prostituting one's daughter. The penalty is burning (ba-esh yisrefu), the only case in this chapter where fire rather than stoning is specified. Whether this reflects a more severe judgment or a different juridical tradition is uncertain; burning after stoning is attested in Josh 7:25. The stated purpose — "and there shall not be depravity [zimmah] in your midst" — makes the communal contagion logic explicit.

**v.17 — chesed hu:** This verse contains one of the most striking uses of the word chesed in the entire Hebrew Bible. Chesed normally denotes "covenant-faithfulness," "lovingkindness," "loyalty" — the characteristic virtue of YHWH toward Israel and of covenant partners toward each other. Here, applied to incest with a sister, it appears to carry a different semantic load: most translators render it "disgrace" or "shame." The scholarly discussion is extensive. The most convincing explanation is that chesed here (the only occurrence with a clearly negative meaning) is being used in a rare technical-legal sense: an act that violates the proper boundaries of hesed — of the loyalty bond — within a family, inverting the family solidarity it names into a transgression against it. Alternatively, it may be a loan sense from a cognate Semitic root meaning "disgrace/reproach." Whatever the etymology, the MT reading must be preserved; the LXX reads *asebema* ("impiety/sin"), removing the linguistic difficulty by substituting a general term.

**v.18 — dovah:** The root dav denotes sickness or languishing; dovah is the woman in the sickness of her menstrual period (niddah, as in v.21, is the noun for the menstrual state of impurity). The offense here is not merely breaking the niddah prohibition of 15:19–24 and 18:19 but doing so mutually — "she has uncovered the flow of her blood" — making her an active participant rather than a passive victim. Both are cut off (kareth). The difference from the death-penalty offenses is that this violation, while serious, does not involve the same radical category confusion as adultery or incest with a parent's spouse.

**v.19 — she'ero:** "His own flesh" — she'er (flesh, near of kin) is the term for close familial relation based on consanguinity. The verse applies to the aunts who are blood relatives of the father or mother (as opposed to the uncle's wife in v.20, who is an aunt by marriage). The penalty is the vague "they shall bear their iniquity" — apparently a divine penalty of consequence rather than specified human execution.

**v.20 — aririn yamut:** "Childless they shall die" — ariri (from the root 'rr, perhaps "stripped bare") denotes a man who dies without heirs. In ancient Israel, childlessness was the effective extinction of a man's name and household from the covenant community and from the land. To die ariri was to be erased from Israel's future. This penalty — which falls short of death but exceeds cutting-off in its dynastic consequence — occupies a distinctive middle tier in the chapter's graduated penalties.

**v.21 — niddah hi:** The wife of a brother who is still living is called a niddah — a term that normally designates menstrual impurity, but here used in the broader sense of something set apart as off-limits, prohibited, unclean. The levirate marriage law (Deut 25:5–10) creates an apparent tension: a man is required to marry his dead brother's wife. The Levitical law here prohibits marriage to a living brother's wife. The rabbis resolved this by distinguishing the cases carefully; Christian exegesis noted the case of Herod Antipas and John the Baptist (Mark 6:18), where John cited this verse as grounds for his condemnation of the marriage.

**v.22 — lo taqia:** "The land shall not vomit you out" — the verb qaya' (to vomit) was introduced in 18:25, 28 as the image of the land's response to defilement. The land is here treated as a moral subject that retches when filled with the violations catalogued in chapters 18 and 20. The image is visceral and deliberate: the land itself is understood to have a holiness-requirement; it expels covenant violators as a body expels poison.

**v.23 — va-aquts bam:** "I was loathed by them" — quts be- means "to feel disgust/loathing toward." The divine emotional register here is significant: YHWH does not simply pronounce judgment on the nations; he was repulsed by their practices. The idiom mirrors the human response to dietary violations (lo teshaqqtsu — "do not make yourselves detestable" in v.25, from shaqats, "to detest"); the nations' practices produced a divine shaqats/qots response. The rhetoric equates the moral disgust of God with the instinctive revulsion of the Israelite toward forbidden foods.

**v.24 — eretz zavat chalav u-devash:** The land-promise formula appears here at the exact hinge between expelling the nations (v.23) and the call to separation (v.25). Its placement is theologically precise: the gift of the land — a land overflowing with milk and honey — is the positive side of the covenant whose negative side is the penalty for defilement. The Hebrew zavat is a Qal participle feminine of zuv (to flow): "a land that flows milk and honey." Chalav (milk) and devash (honey — likely fig-honey/date syrup, not bee-honey in most occurrences) are the paired images of agricultural abundance and pastoral wealth.

**v.24 — hivdalti etkhem:** "I have distinguished you from the peoples" — the verb hivdil (Hiphil of badal, "to separate/distinguish") is the key term of the chapter's closing theology. It appears three times in vv.24–26: God has distinguished Israel (v.24); Israel must distinguish between clean and unclean animals (v.25); God has distinguished Israel to be his own (v.26). The triple occurrence forms an explicit theological argument: God's act of election-distinction grounds Israel's obligation to practice distinction in the dietary laws, which in turn expresses and maintains the people's status as the distinguished/holy people. The separation from the nations is not ethnic isolationism but a covenantal distinctiveness that manifests in daily practice.

**v.25 — lo teshaqtsu et-nafshoteikhem:** "You shall not make your souls detestable [by them]" — the verb shaqats in Hiphil (causative) means "to make detestable/cause to detest." The nefesh here is the whole person as a desiring and tasting subject. By eating what is classified as unclean, the person does not merely break a regulation; they render their own inner self (nefesh) detestable in the sphere of the holy. The dietary laws are thus not arbitrary cultural markers but practices through which the inner person is either oriented toward holiness or corrupted toward defilement.

**v.26 — qedoshim tihyu li:** The holiness formula here adds the crucial word li — "to me." Not only: "be holy [as I am holy]" (as in 19:2) but "be holy to me" — holiness as personal dedication to YHWH, not merely ethical or ritual separation in the abstract. This is the relational ground of the entire Holiness Code: Israel's holiness is not self-referential purity but orientation toward the Holy One who claims them as his own (lihyot li — "to be mine").

**v.27 — ov o yid'oni:** The chapter closes by returning to the topic of v.6 (the person who "turns to" the ovot and yid'onim). The distinction between vv.6 and 27 is legally significant: v.6 addresses the client — the person who consults the medium — and assigns divine kareth; v.27 addresses the practitioner — the man or woman "in whom there is" an ov or yid'oni, i.e., who practices the craft — and assigns human execution by stoning. The same double formula that opened the chapter's penal section (mot yumatu, ragom yirgemu) closes it, forming a literary inclusio with v.2. The phrase "in whom there is" (asher yihyeh vahem) may suggest possession — the medium is inhabited by the spirit — or simply professional practice.

---

## Key Term Table

| Hebrew | Transliteration | EMET Rendering | Notes |
|--------|----------------|----------------|-------|
| מוֹת יוּמָת | mot yumat | "he shall surely be put to death" | Inf. absolute + finite verb; capital sentence |
| רָגוֹם יִרְגְּמוּ | ragom yirgemu | "they shall stone him" (lit. "stoning they shall stone") | Inf. absolute; communal execution by stoning |
| אֲנִי אֶתֵּן אֶת-פָּנַי | ani eten et-panai | "I will set my face" | Divine idiom of hostile personal attention |
| עַם הָאָרֶץ | am ha-aretz | "the people of the land" | Community obligated to enforce covenant holiness |
| דָּמָיו בּוֹ / דְּמֵיהֶם בָּם | damav bo / demeihem bam | "his/their blood is upon him/them" | Formula of self-incurred bloodguilt |
| הַנֶּפֶשׁ | ha-nefesh | "the person/soul" | The individual as moral subject |
| הָאֹבוֹת | ha-ovot | "the mediums" | Necromantic practitioners; cf. 19:31 |
| הַיִּדְּעֹנִים | ha-yid'onim | "the spirits of the dead" | "Knowers"; those with familiar spirits |
| זִמָּה | zimmah | "depravity" | Moral corruption; licentiousness |
| תֶּבֶל | tevel | "confusion" | Violation of categorical order; incest/bestiality |
| חֶסֶד | chesed | "disgrace" (v.17) | Unique negative use; normally "covenant-faithfulness" |
| נִדָּה | niddah | "impurity" (v.21) | Menstrual impurity; by extension, "forbidden/off-limits" |
| עֲרִירִים | aririn | "childless" | Dying without heirs; dynastic extinction |
| לֹא תָקִיא | lo taqia | "shall not vomit you out" | The land as moral subject; cf. 18:25,28 |
| וָאָקֻץ בָּם | va-aquts bam | "I was loathed by them" | Divine disgust at nations' practices |
| אֶרֶץ זָבַת חָלָב וּדְבַשׁ | eretz zavat chalav u-devash | "a land flowing with milk and honey" | The land-promise formula; gift of covenant |
| הִבְדַּלְתִּי אֶתְכֶם | hivdalti etkhem | "I have distinguished you" | Divine election as act of separation |
| וְהִבְדַּלְתֶּם | ve-hivdaltem | "you shall distinguish" | Israel's obligation to practice distinction |
| קְדֹשִׁים תִּהְיוּ לִי | qedoshim tihyu li | "you shall be holy to me" | Holiness as personal dedication; "to me" added |
| לִהְיוֹת לִי | lihyot li | "to be mine" | Covenant possession; Israel as the LORD's own |
| דֹּוָה | dovah | "in the sickness [of her impurity]" | Root dav: languish/sick; menstrual state |
| מִשְׁפַּחְתּוֹ | mishpachto | "his family/clan" | Extended kin group sharing communal guilt |
| מִקְדָּשִׁי | miqdashi | "my sanctuary" | Defiled by moral violation even outside its precincts |

---

## LXX Variants (Codex Vaticanus, pp. 106–107)

**v.2 — Molech:** LXX reads *Moloch* (Μολόχ), a straightforward transliteration of the consonantal form. Notably, the LXX does not interpret or explain the deity. Some LXX manuscripts read *archonti* ("ruler/prince") for Molech in passages where the referent is understood as a title rather than a proper name; Vaticanus appears to preserve the straightforward transliteration here.

**v.3 — "I will set my face against":** LXX *kai episteso to prosopon mou epi ton anthropon ekeinon* — "and I will fix my face upon that man" — rendering the Hebrew idiom precisely. The Greek *prosopon* (face, person) captures the personalized divine hostility of the Hebrew panim phrase.

**v.3 — "to defile my sanctuary":** LXX *mianai ta hagia mou* — "to defile my holy things/sanctuary" — a slightly broader rendering than the MT's singular miqdashi ("my sanctuary"). The LXX may be generalizing to all sacred things rather than the single cultic space.

**v.6 — ha-nefesh:** LXX *he psyche he an* — "the soul which" — standard rendering of ha-nefesh with Greek psyche, which, like the Hebrew, denotes the living person as a whole rather than an immortal soul in the Platonic sense.

**v.9 — "his blood is upon himself":** LXX *to haima autou ep' auton estai* — "his blood shall be upon him" — an exact rendering of damav bo. The phrase became an important legal formula in Greek legal language and appears in the NT (Matt 27:25 in a related construction).

**v.12 — tevel:** LXX *asebema* (ἀσέβημα) — "impiety/sin" — rather than a literal rendering of tevel ("confusion"). The LXX translators appear not to have had a precise equivalent for this rare term and substituted a general moral-failure word. This choice eliminates the categorical-confusion semantic of the Hebrew.

**v.13 — to'evah:** LXX *bdelygma* (βδέλυγμα) — "abomination/detestable thing" — the standard LXX rendering of to'evah throughout. Bdelygma carries a strong sense of physical revulsion and ritual disqualification, closely matching the Hebrew term's semantic range.

**v.17 — chesed hu:** LXX *asebema estin* — "it is impiety/sin" — again substituting a general term for the unusual chesed. The LXX reading erases the linguistic peculiarity entirely. This is significant: the LXX translators either read a variant Hebrew text (perhaps chased = "disgraced") or chose to paraphrase rather than reproduce a term that made no obvious sense in context. The MT's chesed is the lectio difficilior and almost certainly original.

**v.20 — aririn yamut:** LXX *ateknoi apothanountai* — "childless they shall die" — a precise rendering of aririn with Greek ateknos (childless, without children). The Greek exactly preserves the distinctive third-tier penalty.

**v.22 — "the land shall not vomit you out":** LXX *kai ou me prosochthise hymin he ge* — "and the land shall not loathe you" — using prosochthizo (to be indignant/loathe) rather than a vomiting metaphor. The LXX softens the visceral image of the Hebrew (qaya' — to vomit) into a more abstract disgust. This is a consistent LXX tendency: the strong body-based images of Leviticus are moderated in the Greek.

**v.24 — eretz zavat chalav u-devash:** LXX *gen rheousan gala kai meli* — "a land flowing milk and honey" — a standard and consistent rendering. The participle *rheousan* (flowing, from rheo) captures the Hebrew *zavat* (flowing/gushing) with precision.

**v.25 — "you shall not make your souls detestable":** LXX *kai ou prosochthieite tas psychas hymon* — "and you shall not make your souls loathe" — again prosochthizo for the Hebrew shaqats. The Greek preserves the nefesh/psyche correspondence and the reflexive dynamic.

**v.26 — "holy to me / to be mine":** LXX *kai esesthe moi hagioi hoti hagios ego kyrios ho theos hymon ho aphorisas hymas apo panton ton ethnon einai emoi* — "and you shall be holy to me, for holy am I the LORD your God who set you apart from all the nations to be mine." The LXX expands slightly with *ho theos hymon* ("your God") not explicit in the MT at this point, and *apo panton ton ethnon* ("from all the nations") adds "all," where MT has simply "from the peoples." Vaticanus preserves this expanded reading consistently.

**v.27 — "in whom there is a medium or a spirit of the dead":** LXX *en hois estin eggastrimythos e epaoidos* — "in whom there is a ventriloquist or enchanter" — the LXX renders ov as *eggastrimythos* ("belly-speaker/ventriloquist"), reflecting the ancient understanding of the ov medium as one who speaks in a hollow, internal voice (cf. Isa 29:4, where the word of the necromancer is described as coming "from the ground" in a whisper). The yid'oni is rendered *epaoidos* ("enchanter/charmer"). These Greek terms reflect the Hellenistic understanding of the phenomena rather than the Hebrew theological category.

---

*EMET translation completed. Leviticus 20:1–27. All 27 verses rendered. Source: Leningrad Codex folios 70r–70v. LXX: Vaticanus pp. 106–107.*
